summary: DHH reflects on the childhood lesson of putting setbacks in perspective by remembering things could always be worse. He connects this mindset to Stoic philosophy, particularly negative visualization and the idea that we suffer more in our minds than in reality. He argues that resilience comes from controlling our reactions to events rather than the events themselves. He closes by wishing modern culture embraced more of this perspective instead of catastrophizing.
key_points: The 'it could be worse' mindset is a practical form of Stoic negative visualization applied after setbacks occur
We suffer more in our minds than in reality — our reactions to events cause more pain than the events themselves
Resilience is built by recognizing that our reactions are not fixed and can be consciously changed
Modern culture trends toward catastrophizing ('this is the worst thing ever') rather than maintaining perspective
money_quotes: The way to make those setbacks and the inevitable suffering in life bearable was to constantly remind yourself that... it could be worse!
The underlying premise is the essence of Stoicism: We suffer more in our mind than in reality.
The ultimate resilience to face the struggles of living lies in controlling our reactions to the things that happen.
Our reactions are not given. They can be changed.
I wish there was more of that today. More "it could be worse". Less "this is the worst thing ever".
key_insight: Resilience comes not from avoiding hardship but from training yourself to put suffering in perspective — a Stoic practice modern culture has largely abandoned in favor of catastrophizing.
